segments.s716-g49: سپاهی دلاور is a nominal phrase (سپاهی 'army/troop' modified by the adjective دلاور 'brave'). بدیشان is the dative/indirect-object clitic + preposition meaning 'to them'. سپرد is the past-tense verb 'handed over / entrusted' and takes the preceding nominal phrase as its object and بدیشان as indirect object (word order is poetic but follows N V / N + indirect-object pattern).
segments.s716-g50: همه here is a universal quantifier 'all'. نامداران and شیران are plurals naming groups — 'the renowned ones' and metaphorical 'lions' (= valiant men) — joined by و 'and'. گرد functions adverbially 'around / gathered about', indicating the group assembled around the scene.
segments.s716-g51: برآمد is a past/poetic perfect meaning 'rose / issued forth'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. درگاه here means 'the royal court / threshold'. بهرام is the possessor/name and کوس is the noun 'war-drum' (the whole phrase: 'from the court rose Bahram's drum'). Word order places the verb first (typical in poetry) and then the origin and the possessed noun phrase.
segments.s716-g52: رخ here means 'face' or 'surface' (poetically: the visible side). خورشد is a poetic past-form expressing a change of state ('became / shone / was suffused'). از گرد functions 'from/about' (the surrounding / from the dust/around it) and چون introduces a simile 'like'. آبنوس is the simile target ('ebony' → very dark/black). Together the clause says the face/surface took on a quality like ebony.
segments.s716-g53: ز چین روی یکسر is an instrumental/causal phrase: ز + چین روی 'from/with a wrinkle on (his) face' (چین = wrinkle, روی = face). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely / at once', and به ایران نهاد uses نهاد (past of نهادن) meaning 'set/placed' in the sense 'set his course / directed himself toward' — so the clause means 'with a frown he entirely set himself toward Iran.'
segments.s716-g54: به‌روز here means 'on the day' (temporal phrase). سفندار‌مذ is a proper name/title (Sefandar‑maz / Spandarmad); مذ is an old/poetic particle meaning 'said/aforementioned' or 'so-called'. بامداد is 'morning'. Together they form the time specification 'on the morning of the day called Spandarmad.'
segments.s716-g41: فرستاد is a past‑tense verb (3rd sg.) meaning 'sent/dispatched' and precedes its subject here (a common older/poetic word order: verb → subject). خاقان is the subject 'the khagan'. یلان + the enclitic object marker -را is fused into رابخواند (ra‑خواند) — رابخواند is the past verb 'called/summoned' with the object marker attached, so the clause reads roughly 'the khagan summoned the yelan (heroes)'.
segments.s716-g42: This clause describes the manner/result of the summoning. به دیوان 'at/to the court' (به + دیوان), دینار دادن uses دادن in the infinitival/gerund sense 'giving' (an action accompanying the main verb), and نشاند is the past of نشاندن 'to seat/settle' (here archaic past 'he had [them] seated'). Together: 'by giving dinars at the court he seated them.'
segments.s716-g43: چنین گفت مهتر = 'thus said the chief' (چنین 'thus'; گفت here is the verbal past 'said' — the provided senses list doesn't include the verbal sense so treat it as the reporting verb). بدین is a compound prepositional form (ب- as the preposition/prefix + دین here standing for 'this/these' in older usage) meaning 'to these', and هر دو مرد 'both men' specifies the addressees.
segments.s716-g44: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. هشیار باشید is the 2nd‑person plural present/subjunctive used imperatively — 'be alert' or 'be on your guard'. روز نبرد is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the day of battle' (روز + modifier نبرد), giving the time for the instruction.
segments.s716-g45: همیشه 'always' sets frequency. به + proper name (به بهرام) marks the target/person to watch. دارید (2nd‑pl present 'you have/keep') combines with چشم 'eye/gaze' in the idiom چشم داشتن ('to keep watch'), so literally 'you keep an eye on Bahram.'
segments.s716-g46: The correlative construction چه ... چه ... means 'whether ... or ...'. هنگام 'at the time of' + شادی 'joy' and then هنگام + خشم 'anger' specify the two contrasted occasions: 'in times of joy and in times of anger.'
segments.s716-g47: گذرهای جیحون is a noun phrase 'the crossings/passages of the Jeyhun' (گذرهای plural + proper place name). بدارید is 2nd‑person plural present imperative 'keep/maintain', and پاک is the adjective 'clean/pure' complementing what to keep: 'keep the Jeyhun crossings clean.'
segments.s716-g48: ز جیحون 'from the Jeyhun' (ز = 'from'), به گردون 'to the sky/heavens' (به + گردون), برآرید is 2nd‑person plural present/subjunctive used as an exhortation 'raise/lift up', and خاک 'dust/earth' is the object: 'raise dust from the Jeyhun to the sky.'
segments.s716-g33: چو is the archaic/poetic temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. بشنید is the 3rd-person singular past of شنیدن ('he heard'); here its subject follows it (بهرام) in the poetic word order. دل تازه شد is a predication: دل 'heart' + تازه شد 'became fresh/renewed' (شد = past of شدن, 'to become'), so the whole segment reads 'When Bahram heard, his heart became refreshed.'
segments.s716-g34: بخندید is the 3rd-person singular past 'he laughed.' و links the two actions ('and'). بر دیگر اندازه شد is an idiomatic predicate: بر here begins a phrase 'on/upon' + دیگر 'another/other' modifying اندازه 'measure/mode/degree', and شد 'became' — literally 'he became upon another measure,' i.e. 'his demeanor/degree changed' or 'he assumed a different bearing.'
segments.s716-g35: بران is an adverbial/locative 'thereon/at that' (classical usage). برنهادند is past 3rd-person plural 'they set forth/raised/displayed' (بر + نهادند). یکسر گوان is an adverbial phrase: یکسر 'at once/entirely' and گوان 'thus/so' — together 'all at once in that way.' The sequence is poetic (adverbials surrounding the verb).
segments.s716-g36: که here introduces the result/consequence clause 'so that.' بگزید is the verb 'to choose' (گزید) used with a modal necessity that follows; بایـد is the impersonal necessity 'it is necessary / must.' دو مردجوان is the object 'two young men.' The order 'بگزید باید' is an inverted classical word order meaning 'it is necessary to choose.'
segments.s716-g37: که here functions as an interrogative/relative 'which/who.' زیبد is a classical present/predicative form meaning 'befits' or 'is fitting.' بران هر دو is 'for/among those two' (بران = 'for that/thereon', هر دو = 'each/both of the two' — here meaning 'either of the two' or 'which of the two'). بر مهتری literally 'for preeminence/chiefship' (بر = for/toward, مهتری = preeminence/chief position). So the clause asks which of the two is fit for leadership.
segments.s716-g38: همان 'the same' refers back to the previously mentioned person. رنج‌کش is a noun 'one who bears hardship / a hardy toiler' used as the kind of person required. باید 'is necessary / is needed.' و links the next noun; لشکری here names the military role or soldiery ('and as regards the soldiery' or 'and for the army'). The phrase is elliptical: 'the same hardy man is needed, and (needed) for the troops.'
segments.s716-g39: به چین is a classical locative/phrasing meaning 'in that array/rank' (به = in/at; چین here used poetically for 'array/rank'). مهتری 'preeminence/chiefship' is the predicate; بود is the past copula 'was.' حسنوی نام is the postpositive naming: حسنوی is the personal name (here used as 'called Hasanvī') and نام 'name' marks it as 'named.' The whole means 'the chief in that order was called Hasanvī.'
segments.s716-g40: دگر 'another' introduces the second individual. سرکشی here attributes headstrong/rebellious temperament (a noun used adjectivally as 'headstrong'). بود is the past 'was.' زنگوی نام is again name + 'named': زنگوی the personal name (Zangūy) and نام 'name.' So: 'the other, headstrong, was named Zangūy.'
segments.s716-g1: ازان پس چو بشنید is an introductory temporal clause: ازان پس (از آن پس) = 'after that' (literally 'from that afterwards'), چو = 'when', and بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. Word order is subordinate-temporal + verb (typical older Persian). بهرام گرد is the name of the person who heard; گرد here is part of the name (not an independent prepositional element).
segments.s716-g2: کز ... نامه برد is a subordinate content clause introduced by the archaic relative/complimentizer کز = 'that' (equivalent to modern که). ایران به خاقان کسی نامه برد is literal older-Persian order: 'from Iran to the Khaghan someone (a) letter carried' — برد here is the past tense of بردن 'to carry/bring/send' (so: 'had carried/sent').
segments.s716-g3: بیامد (past 'came') is the main verb: 'came'. دمان is a personal name here (so not the common noun 'skirt/hem'), followed by پیش خاقان چین = 'before / in the presence of the Khaghan of China' (پیش = 'before/in front of', چین = 'China' used as country-title).
segments.s716-g4: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'). The following phrase کای مهتر به آفرین is direct speech begun with the vocative کای = 'O'. مهتر = 'chief/leader' (vocative), and به آفرین is an exclamatory phrase ('worthy of praise' / 'praised be' / 'acclamation'). Note that گفت is the reporting verb; the rest is the quoted address.
segments.s716-g5: شنیدم که = 'I heard that' (شنیدم past 1st-person). آن ریمن = 'that Rīman' (آن is the demonstrative 'that' and ریمن is a proper name). بد هنر is a two-word adjectival phrase meaning 'bad of skill' = 'wicked/ill-skilled; by evil craft' describing ریمن.
segments.s716-g6: همی نامه سازد is an archaic/imperfective present used in narrative: همی = continuous/iterative marker 'keeps / continually', نامه سازد = present/subjunctive-archaic form 'he composes/makes letters'. یک اندر دگر is an adverbial phrase 'one within another' = 'one after another' / 'again and again', giving the sense of repetition.
segments.s716-g7: سپاهی دلاور برگزین is an imperative clause: سپاهی (army/men) qualified by دلاور ('valiant, brave'), followed by the command برگزین = 'choose/select!'. ز چین is a prepositional phrase 'from China' modifying which troops to pick.
segments.s716-g8: بدان تا تو را گردد ایران‌زمین: بدان تا = 'thereby / with that, so that' (بدان is a connective here leading into the purpose clause), تا = 'so that/in order that', تو را (تو + را) marks 'for you' as the beneficiary, گردد is the present/subjunctive 'may become' (گردیدن = become/happen), and ایران‌زمین = 'the land of Iran'. The whole clause gives the purpose/result of the command.
segments.s716-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past third‑person singular verb 'heard' (classical word order places the verb before the subject). خاقان is the subject 'the khāqān'. پر اندیشه شد is a predicate: پر 'full (of)' + اندیشه 'thought' with شد 'became' (simple past/become) — together 'became full of thought'.
segments.s716-g18: ورا is the clitic/indirect object 'to him' (referring back to the khāqān). در دل is the prepositional phrase 'in (his) heart' (دل = heart/mind). اندیشه is 'thinking/thought' located in the heart; چون more literally 'like/as' introduces the simile; بیشه is 'thicket/grove'; شد is again the past 'became' — together: 'his thought in his heart became like a thicket.'
segments.s716-g19: بخواند is a past narrative verb 'he called/summoned' here (classical form with ب‑). آنکس‌ان را is the accusative object 'those people' (‑ان marks plurality) with را as the definite/object marker. که بودند پیر is a relative clause 'who were elders/old,' where بودند is past 'they were' and پیر qualifies them as elders/wise men.
segments.s716-g20: This is a noun‑list in apposition describing those summoned: سخنگوی 'speaker/orator', و 'and', داننده 'knower/expert', و 'and', یادگیر 'learner/memoriser'. The list expands on پیر and functions as a coordinated noun phrase (no new verb).
segments.s716-g21: بدیشان is the indirect object 'to them'. بگفت is the past 'he said/told'. آنچ is the relative pronoun 'that which/what'. بهرام گفت is an embedded clause 'Bahram said' (here گفت functions as the past verb 'said'), so the whole segment means 'he told them that which Bahram said.'
segments.s716-g22: همه رازها is the subject 'all the secrets' (رازها = secrets, preceded by همه 'all'). برگشاد is a perfect/past participial/verb form 'opened/revealed' (here active: he revealed or they were revealed). از نهفت literally 'from concealment' — از 'from' + نهفت 'hidden' — meaning 'that had been hidden.'
segments.s716-g23: چنین is the adverb 'thus/so'. یافت is the past 'he found/obtained'. پاسخ is the object 'answer/response'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. فرزانگان is the source noun 'the wise ones/sages.' The clause means 'thus he obtained an answer from the sages.'
segments.s716-g24: ز introduces additional sources: 'from relatives (خویشان) near (نزدیک) and (و) strangers/foreigners (بیگانگان)'. Note adjective نزدیک follows the noun it qualifies (typical word order), so 'خویشان نزدیک' = 'close relatives.'
segments.s716-g9: بگیرم is 1st-person singular subjunctive/optative (used here as a volitional/future: “I will seize / let me seize”). The preposition به is used instrumentally “by/with” (poetic; where Modern Persian would often use با). Word order is verb + instrumental + objects: “I will seize (بگیرم) by the sword (به شمشیر) Iran and Rum.”
segments.s716-g10: This clause has the pattern Subject (تو) + predicate noun (راشاه) + verb (خوانم). خوانم is 1sg present/subjunctive used as “I will call/proclaim.” راشاه is an older fused form meaning “(the) king”; تو is the direct object/beneficiary ‘you’. بران (poetic) = “over/on that” and modifies مرز و بوم (“border and homeland”), so the whole means “I will call you king over that border and land.”
segments.s716-g11: بنام تو is a prepositional phrase “in your name” placed first for emphasis. بر پاسبانان is literal “on/upon the guards” meaning ‘before the sentries’ (locative/scene-setting). به شب is a temporal phrase “at night” (به used here in a temporal/toward sense).
segments.s716-g12: گشایند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive/optative (poetic) meaning “they open” or “they will open.” لب گشودن is an idiom “to open the lip(s)” = to speak/utter; here the action is directed toward two realms: به ایران و توران = “to/for Iran and Turan.” Word order places the indirect/goal phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s716-g13: ببرم is 1st-person subjunctive/optative (here a volitional: “I will take/cut off”). The direct object sequence سر خسرو بی‌هنر places the noun (سر) before the name and epithet (خسرو بی‌هنر) in a compact epic order: “the head of Khosrow the unskilled.”
segments.s716-g14: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که expressing a wished result/purpose. بادا is the optative particle “may it be.” The word مه here is an archaic/poetic form representing the 1st person (my / I) in Ferdowsi’s language (this sense is not in the supplied sense-list, so marked -1). پای and سر are literal “foot” and “head” used metaphorically (positions of dominance/honour). از ایشان = “from/of them.” Overall the clause reads as a wish: “may my foot be upon them and my head among them,” i.e. a boast of domination. (Because مه’s personal/possessive usage is archaic, it is not assignable to the provided senses.)
segments.s716-g15: چون here functions as a temporal/conditional conjunction (“when/if/as”). The phrase من کهتری را places the speaker (من) with a role label or description (کهتری = “the lesser one”) plus the object marker را; ببندم is 1st-person subjunctive/optative “I bind/close” (used here as a conditional/future action), and میان is the locative/adverb “in the midst / between.” The clause therefore reads “if/when I bind the lesser one in the midst.”
segments.s716-g16: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” بن = “root/foundation”; برکنم is 1sg present/subjunctive meaning “I will pluck up / uproot” (literally to take up from the root). تخم ساسانیان = “the seed/stock of the Sasanians” (direct object). The word order places the source phrase before the verb: “from the root I will uproot the Sasanian seed.”
segments.s716-g25: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that'. این is the demonstrative 'this/the foregoing'. کارخوارست is a fused predication (کارخوار + است) meaning something like 'is menial/wage‑earning/lowly' with the copula -st (third person). و links the two predicates; دشوارنیز is دشوار ('difficult') with the enclitic نیز ('also'), so the clause says 'this is menial and also difficult'.
segments.s716-g26: که again introduces the content clause 'that...'. بر + تخم ساسان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the seed/stock of Sasan' (ساسان is a proper name in genitive position). پرآمد is a past verb 'arose/appeared/befell' placed before its subject in poetic word order; قفیز (an uncommon/obscure noun here) is the thing that 'appeared' or 'befell' the seed.
segments.s716-g27: ولیکن is a classical adversative 'but'. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرم here refers to the leader Bahram (proper name in the context); راند is the past verb 'drove/led/commanded', and سپاه is the object 'army/host' (verb + object order is somewhat flexible in poetry). The clause therefore means 'but when Bahram led the army'.
segments.s716-g28: نماید is a 3rd‑person form of 'نمودن' here meaning 'shows / gives'. خردمند is the agent 'the wise man'; را is the definite/object marker attached to خردمند (an instance of poetic word order/emphasis). رای و راه are the two direct objects 'counsel (رای)' and 'the way/method (راه)'. The sense is 'the wise man offers counsel and direction.'
segments.s716-g29: به ایران is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in/among Iran (the land or people)'. بسی is a classical quantifier 'much/many' (archaic in form). دوستدارش is 'his supporters/admirers' (دوستدار + possessive -ش), and بود is the past copula 'was/there were' — together meaning 'he had many supporters in Iran.'
segments.s716-g30: چو is 'when/for/as'. خاقان is the title 'khaghan' (a supreme ruler). یکی here functions as 'one (as it were)/a certain' or 'at once' in an idiomatic sense; خوی + ش = خویش ('his kinsman') with the possessive split across two tokens; و yārsh = 'and his companion/ally'. بود is past 'was'. The clause says the khaghan was both his kinsman and his ally.
segments.s716-g31: برآید is a verb meaning 'arise/come about' (here used optatively/future‑oriented: 'may ... come about' / 'will come about'). ببخت تو = 'your fortune/luck' (word order inversion: the verb precedes the possessor for emphasis). این کار زود places the matter and the adverb 'soon' after it: 'this affair soon' — yielding 'may your fortune bring this affair about soon' or 'your luck will soon make this happen.'
segments.s716-g32: سخنهای is the plural/collective 'words/speeches' and بهرام is the possessive proper name 'Bahram' (so: 'Bahram's words'). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and شنود is the present/subjunctive form of 'شنیدن' used after باید to give an impersonal necessity: 'it is necessary to hear' or idiomatically 'one must hear.' Combined: 'Bahram’s words must be heard.'

sentences.s716-s25: He entrusted to them a troop of brave warriors; all the famed men and the valiant (lions) gathered round.
sentences.s716-s26: From the court rose Bahram's war-drum; the face (surface) gleamed from about, like ebony.
sentences.s716-s27: With a single wrinkle on his brow he wholly turned toward Iran, on the morning of the day called Spandarmad.
sentences.s716-s21: The khagan summoned the heroes and, giving dinars at the court, had them seated.
sentences.s716-s22: Thus the chief said to these two men: 'Be alert on the day of battle.'
sentences.s716-s23: Always keep an eye on Bahram, whether in times of joy or in times of anger.
sentences.s716-s24: Keep the crossings of the Jeyhun clean; raise dust from the Jeyhun up to the sky.
sentences.s716-s17: When Bahram heard, his heart was renewed; he laughed and took on a different bearing.
sentences.s716-s18: They displayed them there all at once in that fashion, so that two young men had to be chosen.
sentences.s716-s19: Which of the two would be fit for the supremacy — the same hardy toiler is required, and for the soldiery as well.
sentences.s716-s20: In that rank the preeminence belonged to one called Hasnavī; the other, headstrong, was named Zangūy.
sentences.s716-s1: After that, when Bahram Gird heard that someone from Iran had carried a letter to the Khaghan.
sentences.s716-s2: Doman came before the Khaghan of China and said to him, 'O chief, (worthy of) praise...'
sentences.s716-s3: I heard that that Rīman, ill-skilled in craft, keeps composing letters one after another.
sentences.s716-s4: Select valiant troops from China, so that the land of Iran may become yours.
sentences.s716-s9: When the khāqān heard it, he became full of thought, and his thinking in his heart became like a thicket.
sentences.s716-s10: He summoned those people who were elders — speakers, knowers, and memorisers.
sentences.s716-s11: He told them what Bahram had said; he revealed all the secrets that had been hidden.
sentences.s716-s12: Thus he obtained an answer from the sages, from close relatives and from strangers.
sentences.s716-s5: I will seize Iran and Rum by the sword, and I will proclaim you king over that border and land.
sentences.s716-s6: In your name, at night before the sentries, they will open their lips to Iran and Turan.
sentences.s716-s7: I will cut off the head of Khosrow the unskilled — may my foot be upon them and my head among them.
sentences.s716-s8: If I bind the lesser one in the midst, I will uproot the Sasanian seed from its root.
sentences.s716-s13: For this is menial work and also hard — that a qafiz fell upon the seed (lineage) of Sasan.
sentences.s716-s14: But when Bahram led the host, the wise man gave counsel and showed the way.
sentences.s716-s15: He had many supporters in Iran, for the khaghan was at once his kinsman and his ally.
sentences.s716-s16: May your luck bring this about soon; Bahram’s words must be heard.
